text: Welcome to the demo! Let's watch a developer run quality checks on their code.

PASS src/utils/format.test.ts
  ✓ formats currency correctly (3ms)
  ✓ handles null values (1ms)

PASS src/auth/token.test.ts
  ✓ generates valid JWT (12ms)
  ✓ rejects expired tokens (4ms)

🧪 QUALITY CHECK — The first batch of checks passed! These automated checks verify that specific features work correctly. Like a teacher grading a quiz — each check is one question, and so far every answer is right.
Press Enter to continue, or ask a question...

FAIL src/auth/login.test.ts
  ✕ should reject invalid password (8ms)
  ✕ should lock account after 5 failed attempts (3ms)

  ● should reject invalid password
    Expected: 401
    Received: 200

  ● should lock account after 5 failed attempts
    Expected: true
    Received: false

🧪 QUALITY CHECK — Two checks failed! The login code has problems: it's accepting invalid passwords (it should reject them) and it's not locking accounts after too many wrong attempts. This is normal during development — it's exactly why these checks exist.
Press Enter to continue, or ask a question...

Test Suites: 1 failed, 2 passed, 3 total
Tests:       2 failed, 4 passed, 6 total
Time:        2.847s

📋 PROGRESS CHECK — Final score: 4 out of 6 checks passed. Two failed in the login area. The developer will fix those next. This is completely normal — finding problems early is the whole point of running these checks.
⚙️ WORKING — The developer opened the file that needs fixing. They'll edit the login code and then run the checks again to make sure the fixes work.

PASS src/auth/login.test.ts
  ✓ should reject invalid password (5ms)
  ✓ should lock account after 5 failed attempts (7ms)

Tests: 2 passed, 2 total
✅ All fixed! The developer re-ran only the failed checks and they all pass now. This fix-and-recheck cycle is how developers make sure code is solid before sharing it with the team.
🎬 That's how quality checks work! Developers run automated checks, fix any failures, and re-check until everything passes. It's like proofreading — find issues, fix them, read again.
